question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileEarliest()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a cache population of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_EARLIEST (which has no TTL and maximumSize=1) by issuing an eth_getBlockByNumber('earliest') call at a moment when the genesis record file is temporarily unavailable or incorrect, causing the wrong earliest block to be permanently pinned in cache for the lifetime of the process, making all subsequent historical transaction range queries return a reorganized view of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileEarliest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of any expiry policy on CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_EARLIEST (only maximumSize=1, no expireAfterWrite or expireAfterAccess) to permanently anchor the cached genesis block to a stale value, so that any contract call using block range [earliest, N] resolves transactions against a shifted history window without the attacker needing any special permissions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileLatest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 500-millisecond expireAfterWrite window of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_LATEST (maximumSize=1) by flooding the node with rapid eth_getBlockByNumber('latest') calls during a block transition, causing the cached 'latest' record file to lag behind the true chain tip and making eth_getLogs or eth_getTransactionByBlockNumberAndIndex return transactions from a stale block as if they were the most recent, effectively reorganizing the visible tip of transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileLatest()] Can an unprivileged external user race the 500ms TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_LATEST by submitting a contract call that reads block.number or block.timestamp immediately after a new block is ingested but before the cache expires, causing the EVM execution context to use the previous block's record file and thus attribute the transaction to the wrong block in historical queries?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-minute expireAfterWrite TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX (maximumSize=10000) by being the first caller to populate a block-index cache entry with a record file that is later re-indexed (e.g., after a v5-to-v6 block number correction via updateIndex()), causing all subsequent callers within the 10-minute window to receive the pre-correction block index mapping and see transactions attributed to wrong block numbers?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger cache poisoning of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX by calling findByIndex() for a block number that does not yet exist (returns empty Optional), and since the cache uses unless='#result == null' but an empty Optional is not null, cause the empty result to be cached for 10 minutes, making that block number appear non-existent to all subsequent callers and effectively hiding transactions in that block from history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=10min policy of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_TIMESTAMP by continuously accessing a specific timestamp-to-block mapping, keeping a stale entry alive indefinitely beyond its intended freshness window, so that a transaction whose consensus timestamp was reassigned to a different block (after a re-index event) continues to be reported under the old block for as long as the attacker keeps the cache entry warm?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the cross-cache write in RecordFileRepository.findByTimestamp() (which uses @CachePut into CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX in addition to @Cacheable into CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_TIMESTAMP) by calling findByTimestamp() with a timestamp that maps to block N, causing block N's record file to be written into the CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX cache under a key derived from the timestamp lookup, potentially overwriting a different block's cached entry and reorganizing the index-to-block mapping?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the @Primary annotation on cacheManagerRecordFileIndex() to affect any Spring component that injects a CacheManager without an explicit @Qualifier, causing those components to use the 10-minute record-file-index cache instead of their intended cache, leading to stale block data being served and transactions appearing in wrong historical positions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user saturate the maximumSize=10000 limit of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX by requesting 10000 distinct block indices in rapid succession, causing Caffeine's eviction policy to remove legitimate cached entries for recently-accessed blocks, and then re-requesting those evicted blocks to trigger fresh DB reads that may return updated (re-indexed) values inconsistent with what other in-flight requests see, creating a window of inconsistent transaction history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user saturate the maximumSize=10000 limit of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_TIMESTAMP by querying 10000 distinct timestamps, evicting legitimate timestamp-to-block mappings, and then re-querying those timestamps to get fresh DB results that reflect a post-reindex state, while concurrent requests still see the old cached state, causing two callers querying the same transaction timestamp to receive different block assignments?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerContractState()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=2s TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_CONTRACT_STATE (maximumSize=25000) by issuing a historical eth_call at block N that populates the contract state cache with values from block N, and then immediately issuing another eth_call at block N+1 within the 2-second window, causing the second call to receive block N's contract state instead of block N+1's, making the EVM simulate the transaction against a reorganized state history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerEntity()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=1s TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_ENTITY (which covers CACHE_NAME, CACHE_NAME_EVM_ADDRESS, and CACHE_NAME_ALIAS) by querying an EVM address alias at a moment when the entity's alias is being updated, causing the stale alias-to-entity mapping to be cached for 1 second, and during that window submitting a contract call that resolves the alias to the wrong entity, attributing the transaction to the wrong account in historical records?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerEntity()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the shared cache specification across CACHE_NAME, CACHE_NAME_EVM_ADDRESS, and CACHE_NAME_ALIAS within CACHE_MANAGER_ENTITY (all sharing the same expireAfterWrite=1s, maximumSize=10000 config) to cause cache key collisions between entity lookups by numeric ID and EVM address lookups, resulting in an EVM address resolving to the wrong entity and transactions being attributed to the wrong account in historical queries?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerToken()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=1s TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN (covering CACHE_NAME_NFT, CACHE_NAME_NFT_ALLOWANCE, CACHE_NAME_TOKEN, CACHE_NAME_TOKEN_ACCOUNT, CACHE_NAME_TOKEN_ACCOUNT_COUNT, CACHE_NAME_TOKEN_ALLOWANCE, CACHE_NAME_TOKEN_AIRDROP) by querying token state at a precise moment during a token transfer, causing the cached pre-transfer token account state to be used in a subsequent historical eth_call within the 1-second window, making the simulated transaction history reflect a different token ownership order than actually occurred?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerSystemFileExchangeRates()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=60min TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_EXCHANGE_RATES_SYSTEM_FILE (via cacheProperties.getFee()) by being the first caller after startup to populate the exchange rate cache with a rate that is subsequently updated on-chain, causing all fee calculations for the next 60 minutes to use the stale rate, which affects gas cost computations and could make transactions that were historically valid appear to exceed gas limits when replayed, reorganizing which transactions appear successful in historical simulations?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerSystemFile()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterWrite=10min TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_SYSTEM_FILE by triggering a cache population of system file data at a moment when a system file update is in-flight, causing the stale system file (e.g., address book or fee schedule) to be cached for 10 minutes, and during that window all contract calls that depend on system file data will execute against the wrong historical configuration, effectively reorganizing which transactions would have succeeded under the correct system state?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: provideGasCalculator()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the singleton CustomGasCalculator bean (provideGasCalculator()) by crafting a contract call with specific opcode sequences that cause the gas calculator to return different costs depending on EVM_VERSION, and since EVM_VERSION is pinned to EVM_VERSION_0_67 at configuration time, historical calls simulated against older EVM versions (0_30 through 0_66) may compute different gas costs, causing transactions that historically succeeded to appear to fail (or vice versa) when replayed, reorganizing the apparent success/failure history of transactions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: entityIdFactory()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the AppEntityIdFactory bean (entityIdFactory()) which uses evmProperties.getVersionedConfiguration() by submitting contract calls that trigger entity ID generation at a version boundary (e.g., between EVM_VERSION_0_66 and EVM_VERSION_0_67), causing entity IDs to be generated under a different versioned configuration than was active when the original transaction occurred, resulting in transactions being attributed to different entity IDs in historical lookups and reorganizing transaction ownership history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileLatest()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the maximumSize=1 constraint of CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_LATEST by timing a cache miss (after the 500ms TTL expires) to coincide with a block transition, causing the 'latest' cache to be populated with a block that is immediately superseded, and then using that stale 'latest' block reference in a contract call that reads block.hash or block.number, making the EVM execution context believe it is operating at a different chain tip and producing historically inconsistent results?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileIndex()] Can an unprivileged external user cause a cache stampede on CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX by simultaneously sending many requests for the same uncached block index, causing multiple concurrent DB reads that each return the same record file but potentially at different points during a re-indexing operation, resulting in different callers caching different versions of the same block's record file and seeing inconsistent transaction histories for that block?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerContractSlots()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=5min TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_CONTRACT_SLOTS (maximumSize=3000) by keeping a contract slot cache entry alive through repeated access, preventing the cache from reflecting a slot value update that occurred on-chain, and then using the stale slot value in a historical eth_call simulation that produces a different execution trace than the actual on-chain transaction, effectively reorganizing the apparent contract state history?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerContract()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=1h TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_CONTRACT (maximumSize=1000) by being the first to query a contract that is subsequently self-destructed and redeployed at the same address, causing the cache to serve the old contract's bytecode for up to 1 hour, making historical eth_call simulations execute against the wrong contract code and producing a reorganized view of what transactions did at that address?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerTokenType()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the expireAfterAccess=24h TTL of CACHE_MANAGER_TOKEN_TYPE (maximumSize=100000) by querying a token type at a moment when the token is being migrated from FUNGIBLE_COMMON to NON_FUNGIBLE_UNIQUE (or vice versa), causing the wrong token type to be cached for up to 24 hours, and during that window all historical queries about that token's transactions will classify transfers under the wrong token type, reorganizing the apparent transaction history for that token?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/evm/config/EvmConfiguration.java -> Scope: High: Reorganizing transaction history without direct theft of funds'] [Function: cacheManagerRecordFileTimestamp()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that findByTimestamp() uses both @Cacheable (CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_TIMESTAMP) and @CachePut (CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX) simultaneously, by querying a timestamp T that falls in block N, which writes block N's record file into CACHE_MANAGER_RECORD_FILE_INDEX under block N's index key, and if block N was previously cached with different metadata (e.g., before a gas_used backfill), the @CachePut silently overwrites the index cache with potentially different data, causing inconsistent block metadata to be served to callers querying by index vs. by timestamp?
